title: Tanacetum × cuneifolium
taxonomy: Tanacetum × cuneifolium, commonly known as tansy, is classified as a hybrid plant. It is a cross between the perennial Tanacetum vulgare and possibly another tanacetum species. The classification is still not universally agreed upon by botanists. Tanacetum × cuneifolium belongs to the Asteraceae family. This classification is consistent with its physical characteristics and growth patterns.
uses: Tanacetum × cuneifolium is also known as Tansy, and it has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. The plant has been used to treat digestive issues, such as bloating and gas, due to its carminative properties. Tansy has also been used to reduce fever and relieve pain. However, its use is not recommended due to potential toxicity and the risk of seizures when consumed in large quantities. More research is needed to fully understand the medicinal uses of Tanacetum × cuneifolium.
benefits: Tanacetum × cuneifolium, also known as a Tansy, is often used in herbal medicine due to its potential health benefits. The plant contains compounds such as thujone, which may have anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties. Some studies suggest that Tanacetum × cuneifolium may help to reduce fever, alleviate symptoms of digestive issues, and even exhibit antiseptic properties, making it a potential remedy for wound healing. However, it is essential to note that excessive consumption of the plant can be toxic due to its thujone content. More research is needed to confirm the full extent of Tanacetum × cuneifolium's health benefits.
properties: Tanacetum × cuneifolium, also known as tansy, has been traditionally used for its therapeutic properties, including its ability to repel insects and relieve digestive issues. The plant contains compounds such as camphor, borneol, and thujone, which are believed to have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. Tansy has been used to treat various health issues, including fever, headache, and respiratory problems. However, it's essential to note that tansy can be toxic in large quantities, and its use should be approached with caution. More research is needed to fully understand the therapeutic potential of Tanacetum × cuneifolium.
constituents: Tanacetum × cuneifolium is a plant in the daisy family and its active constituents are primarily responsible for its medicinal properties. The main active constituents of this plant include sesquiterpene lactones, which have been shown to have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial effects. These compounds, along with other bioactive molecules, contribute to the plant's therapeutic potential. In addition to sesquiterpene lactones, Tanacetum × cuneifolium also contains other secondary metabolites such as flavonoids and phenolic acids. The combination of these compounds gives the plant its diverse range of biological activities.
parts: Tanacetum × cuneifolium, also known as tansy, is primarily used for its medicinal properties. The plant's flowers, leaves, and roots are used in traditional medicine, with the flowers being the most commonly used part. The flowers are rich in sesquiterpene lactones, which are responsible for the plant's medicinal properties. The plant has been used to treat a variety of ailments, including digestive issues and skin conditions. The medicinal properties of Tanacetum × cuneifolium are also believed to have antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory effects.
preparations: Tanacetum × cuneifolium herbal preparations are typically made from the plant's leaves and flowers, which are rich in sesquiterpene lactones. These compounds are known for their potential anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. Herbalists often use Tanacetum × cuneifolium to create teas, tinctures, and infusions that can be used to treat various health issues, including digestive problems and skin irritations. side_effects: Tanacetum × cuneifolium, commonly known as pyrethrum, may cause skin irritation, allergic reactions, or digestive issues in some individuals. The plant's essential oil, extracted from its flowers, can be toxic if ingested. It may also cause eye irritation, respiratory problems, or other allergic reactions in people sensitive to its compounds. Prolonged exposure to the plant's oil can lead to liver damage or other health issues. Ingesting large amounts of the plant can cause more severe side effects, including seizures and other neurological problems.
